cards.front: Where is I/O functionality defined in C++?
cards.back: In the C++ standard library (not in core C++), residing in the std namespace. You access it by including the <iostream> header.

cards.text: To use the iostream classes, you must {{c1::#include <iostream>}} at the top of your file.

cards.front: What is a stream in C++?
cards.back: A sequence of bytes that can be accessed sequentially. A stream may produce or consume potentially unlimited amounts of data over time.

cards.text: {{c1::Input streams}} hold input from a data producer (keyboard, file, network), while {{c2::output streams}} hold output for a data consumer (monitor, file, printer).

cards.back: Extraction operator - used with input streams to remove values from the stream
Insertion operator - used with output streams to put values into the stream

cards.text: The three main stream classes are: {{c1::istream}} for input, {{c2::ostream}} for output, and {{c3::iostream}} for bidirectional I/O.
The four standard stream objects in C++ are: {{c1::std::cin}} (input), {{c2::std::cout}} (output), {{c3::std::cerr}} (unbuffered error), and {{c4::std::clog}} (buffered error).

cards.front: What is the difference between std::cerr and std::clog?
cards.back: std::cerr provides unbuffered output (handled immediately), while std::clog provides buffered output (stored and written as a block). Both write to standard error.

cards.front: What standard input device is std::cin typically tied to?

cards.concept: Why the iostream library can safely use multiple inheritance
cards.explanation: Although the iostream class hierarchy uses multiple inheritance (which is normally avoided), the library has been extensively designed and tested to avoid typical multiple inheritance problems like the diamond problem.
examples: iostream derives from both istream and ostream
This allows bidirectional I/O without issues
Safe to use without worrying about MI problems

cards.front: What is std::ios a typedef for?
cards.back: std::basic_ios<char> - it defines functionality common to both input and output streams.
